ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle soup class (FOODON_03311069); no specific kimchi ramyeon subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated kimchi ramyeon entry. A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for kimchi ramyeon or Korean noodle soup at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of kimchi ramyeon is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of spicy noodle broth with kimchi, grilled pork belly, a soft-boiled lava egg, tofu, and enoki mushrooms.

nutrient_source.title: Instant noodles, dry (Korean-style) and composite Korean noodle soup — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for instant ramen noodles, kimchi, pork belly, egg, tofu, and enoki mushrooms, weighted by typical serving composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for restaurant-style kimchi ramyeon as served at Ajumma's. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for: instant ramen noodles (FDC 2103697), kimchi (FDC 169999), pork belly cooked (FDC 168289), hard-boiled egg (FDC 173424), firm tofu (FDC 172475), and enoki mushrooms (FDC 169403). Approximate component weights per 550 g serving: noodles 100 g, broth 250 g, kimchi 60 g, pork belly 70 g, egg 50 g, tofu 50 g, enoki 20 g. Per-100 g values derived from weighted average of components normalised to 100 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of instant noodles and wheat noodle products — analogous estimate based on instant noodles GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for restaurant-style kimchi ramyeon. GI estimated at 55 based on instant noodles (GI range 46–58, mean ~52–55) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. The GI of 55 is categorized as 'low'. The kimchi, pork belly, egg, and tofu components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 57.75) / 100 ≈ 31.8, rounded to 31.7. Carbohydrate per serving = 10.5 g/100 g × 550 g = 57.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~2640 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid kimchi ramyeon due to its very high sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g, equating to ~2640 mg per 550 g serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; consuming the broth sparingly or requesting reduced-sodium preparation can partially mitigate risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with kimchi ramyeon as its low GI (~55) is offset by a high glycemic load per serving (~32), which can still cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise due to the total carbohydrate content; portion control, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, and choosing a smaller noodle portion are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, saturated fat from pork belly, and moderate cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit kimchi ramyeon due to its combination of high sodium (~2640 mg per serving), saturated fat from grilled pork belly (~8.25 g per serving), and moderate dietary cholesterol (~302 mg per serving), all of which are associated with elevated cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Ramyeon (김치 라면) is a Korean spicy noodle soup dish combining instant ramyeon noodles cooked in a fiery, umami-rich broth with fermented kimchi, grilled pork belly, a soft-boiled lava egg, silken or firm tofu, and enoki mushrooms. The dish originates from Korean culinary tradition and has become widely popular across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore where it is served at Korean restaurants such as Ajumma's. The broth is characteristically bold, spicy, and sour from the fermented kimchi, with a deep savoury backbone from the pork and seasoning sachets. The noodles are springy and chewy, the pork belly is tender with a slight char, and the lava egg contributes a creamy yolk texture. Nutritionally, kimchi ramyeon is a moderate-energy dish (~539 kcal per 550 g serving) with meaningful protein (~32 g per serving) from pork, egg, and tofu, but is notably high in sodium (~2640 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index (~55) and high glycemic load (~32) per serving require portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Kimchi provides probiotic benefit and dietary fibre. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians in its standard form, though vegetarian substitutions are available.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kimchi ramen?
Is kimchi ramyeon high in sodium?
What is the difference between ramyeon and ramen?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for restaurant-style kimchi ramyeon as served at Ajumma's Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by a weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for the individual ingredients. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (instant noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the component-based estimation is methodologically sound but introduces uncertainty from variable restaurant portion sizes and preparation methods.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation and is not a primary nutrient of concern for this dish type. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for all components used in this estimation. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the source database entries used. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from component-weighted USDA SR Legacy data and carry moderate uncertainty. The dish is served at Ajumma's Funan, Singapore (field survey listing). Vegetarian option is available per restaurant description. Halal status is not confirmed. The pork belly component makes the standard version non-halal and non-kosher. GI source match is analogous_estimate based on instant noodles GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for kimchi ramyeon was found in approved sources.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input seed record. Estimated nutrients via USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component-weighted method. Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous instant noodles data. Added Korean local name and Chinese simplified/traditional names. Populated all mandatory sections.

Updated gi_source.source_match_notes, health_context.type2_diabetes.key_factor, health_context.type2_diabetes.guidance, and llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect the 'low' GI category while maintaining 'moderate' concern due to high glycemic load. Verified aliases_by_language consistency against flat aliases list. Verified all -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Verified zero-macro justification not needed. Verified ontology match_type values are within allowed set. Confirmed all hard gates pass and score is 8/8.
